hero.title: Sprint one.
hero.tagline: From a plan on paper to a working loop.
hero.lead: Two weeks ago Neo was a write-up. Today the whole path runs: speak a request, Claude drafts it, a real reviewer signs off in a clean web app, and it comes back to you to approve — with no Jira or Git jargon ever reaching the people using it.

summary.title: The loop runs end to end.
body: The proof-of-concept module — Proposals — now goes the whole distance. You can narrate the loop with a single command, and with real keys it opens a branch, drafts with Claude, files a pull request, and moves the ticket to In Review on its own.

The big new piece this sprint is the reviewer web app: a clean dashboard where a contracted expert reviews a draft and clicks Approve or Request Changes. They never see a Jira key, a branch name, or a line of code — just the work.
summary.pull: Plan → scaffold → live integrations → a reviewer app. The skeleton now has a heartbeat.

items.label: Repo scaffolded
items.detail: The Neo loop, router, skill loader, the Proposals module, the private data layer, config, and docs — the full skeleton, ~770 lines.

items.label: Live GitHub
items.detail: Neo opens a real branch, makes a commit, and files a pull request entirely on its own — 245 lines of integration code.

items.label: Live Claude
items.detail: Drafting runs through the Anthropic Messages API, so the first draft of every proposal is written by AI.

items.label: Jira + GitHub + Claude wired
items.detail: All three integrations run as one path under ticket NEO-4 — ticket in, branch + draft + PR out, status updated.

items.label: The --live switch
items.detail: One flag flips Neo from safe dry-run narration to real calls, and fails gracefully with a clear message when a key is missing.

items.label: Reviewer web app
items.detail: A dashboard + reviewer screens (~880 lines) wired to live Jira. Approve, Request Changes, and Re-prompt all work — and no keys, branches, or jargon ever leak to the reviewer.

loop.title: One clean loop, now live.
loop.intro: Every request follows the same five steps. As of this sprint, all five actually run — not as a sketch, but against real Jira, GitHub, and Claude.

steps.name: Speak
steps.desc: A request becomes a tracked ticket on the Jira board.

steps.name: Sort
steps.desc: Neo tags the area and urgency; hot items start, the rest queue.

steps.name: Draft
steps.desc: Claude drafts the work; Neo opens a branch and a pull request.

steps.name: Review
steps.desc: An expert reviews it in the new web app and signs off — or sends it back.

steps.name: Approve
steps.desc: It comes to you for the final yes. Reject and it loops back.

loop.loopNote: Every step in this loop now has working code behind it. Reject still loops the work back to “in progress,” the same way a control system re-runs until the target is met.

demo.title: What I'll show you.
demo.intro: Two things to watch, in order. The first needs nothing but the terminal; the second is the new web app.

items.name: The loop narrated
items.desc: Run the proposals loop on the Red Cross example. The hot item auto-starts, the router picks the module, the nonprofit skill loads, Claude drafts, a branch + PR open, and the ticket moves to In Review.

items.name: The unified dashboard
items.desc: Open the dashboard. Type a request in the chat bar, watch it land on the board, then play the reviewer — see a draft pulled from live Jira and click Approve or Request Changes, all on one page.

items.name: The real thing
items.desc: Same loop, real keys: python -m neo --live opens an actual branch, draft, and pull request. Shown only if we want to commit a live run.

items.label: Commit the reviewer app
items.detail: It's built and working locally — next is landing it on the board as its own tickets and cleaning it up.

items.label: End-to-end live run
items.detail: Take one real proposal all the way through with live keys, start to approved.

items.label: Reviewer notifications
items.detail: Tell the expert there's something to review — on-call ping, message, or dashboard, per contract.

items.label: Second module
items.detail: Drop the same loop onto Landlord or Air Force web dev to prove it generalizes.

footer.credit: Standup by Mariah · Arlington, VA · June 15, 2026
